title: A River Called Anywhere
content:
A river called "anywhere" traverses an unknown prairie
Confined by its size and its will
Through valleys and hills it wanders
Longing for the mighty Ocean

I sit upon the fallen trunk of a fearsome Oak
I surrender to the current of the narrow brook
Adrift in search of a foreign sea
Longing for emancipation

A seagull flashes its brilliant wings overhead
Hope strikes the deepest corridors of my heart
The stream follows my strides onward
The graceful bird rides on a stream of salty perfume

I feel the stream widen and fall still
The delta reveals an infinite sea of diamonds and pearls
The undertow carries me wherever I command
I look up at the seagull and extend to it a smile of gratitude

I am free at last.

title: Call of the Wild
content:
Marble and lumber comprise mighty halls
People do slumber behind stucco walls

Dark ears fall deaf to the tune of a bird
Perverse in the manner of words overheard

To run and to yell is to set free desire
To hasten to fall, to fall to the mire

Above the rustled and chirping birds fly
An unheard attempt to pierce through the sky

A blanket, a layer, to keep safe our home
At night, a shroud to protect those who roam

Aloof from the river, whose melody cries
For faraway places where tall mountains rise

And fall to the valleys, so green and so vast
Where trickling rivers descend oh-so fast

The sun paints the wheat hues of sulfur and cream
The moonlight reflects off a forgotten stream

As dusk briskly frisks the warm-water weeds
Nature inquires what man only needs

To live and to thrive, to find what's been hidden
To saddle up horses not meant to be ridden

Awake are the pieces of nature's great puzzle
Didactic and yearning to break off its muzzle

Unsilenced, the woods host a chorus of voices
With no aching worry for duties and choices

But the wind shares its secrets with no earthly child,
For only a human hears the call of the wild.

title: Does the Moon Also Fall?
content:
The Earth is revolving,
its children evolving,
its forests dissolving
its valor with cries,
and to my surprise,
the sun's embers rise,
to glow and to stumble
while ocean waves tumble
and mountaintops crumble
and hushed voices call,
a question to all,
does the moon also fall?

title: Eden
content:
I look at the stars
The stars do the same
I promise them wonder
They promise me fame

A promise in Heaven
Falls deaf to my call
For nobody listens
To teardrops so small

They might be called miracles
They might be called saints
They might just as easy
Be called golden paints

A blind man can see
An alloy improper
For a plating of platinum
Is still only copper

title: Forgotten Highway
content:
A faded golden line divides an endless black strand,
carelessly applied to an otherwise vacant landscape
A forgotten highway connects two distant towns,
but nobody is certain which town lies on the other side.

Some choose to believe a paradise lies at the end of the line
Others suggest that the road leads to a towering cliff overlooking a polluted sea
Some wonder if the forgotton highway eventually returns to its beginning
A few question whether or not the road even exists to begin with.

An intellectual drought sweeps over the forgotten town on the forgotten highway
The citydwellers relocate underground to affirm their imagined limits
The people look inside themselves for the answers they will never find,
for the city on the other side of the hill has done just the same.

title: Gone To Flowers
content:
How many hours jam between noon and one
Time slowly chases, but you needn't run

From one to two, time passes through
Some passed away, while others grew

By three, the hours come and go
For future hours dare to row

At four, the minutes disappear
While hours only persevere

At five, you know the night is near
As twilight birds sing in your ear

By six, you know it's half-way done
Oh, the good times, back at one!

By seven, dusk still lingers by
You've tried to stop it, I know, you try.

But eight means four are left to pass
By now, it's getting pretty fast

Nine is here, the sky is dark
The bells chime on. Listen! Hark!

Ten, you must slow down.

Eleven, turn around!

Twelve.

title: Hello, Dear Helsinki
content:
Hello, Dear Helsinki, you gem of the North
The Gulf and the Baltic a-slappin' your shore
The forest's green beauty like emeralds galore
Who ever could ask for much more?

By train or by tram or by bus I commute
Uncovering Finland's dear unbridled loot
The city connected by spider-web tracks
I'm dreading the day I go back

I walk to a Cafe for coffee at noon
The scent of fresh spice rolls parades through the room
A quiet young woman reads Albert Camus
What ever is a young mant o do?

At night, people dance in the late summer glow
Whirling and twirling, in circles they go
Afraid not of greed's hand or onlooking eyes
Their romance, a golden surprise

Aboard the great ferry to Tallinn I go
In the summer, untouched by a lone flake of snow
Awake, but still weary from nights spent in town
Where the summer sun never goes down

So remember the things that you'll never forget
For the lights haven't gone out on Broadway, just yet
Don't make plans for the future before they're for sure
'Cause you'll never know what is in store

title: Hepner Hall
content:
Above me, airplanes weave through clouds
like needles through cotton balls,
and columns of lamplight wisp calmly through trees
whose leaves sift through streams of warm winter air.

Round dew drops the size of a quarter
sit softly on kale-green leaves,
ready to burst
at the slightest touch.

I stumble into an old friend
who shows me the way
when, for a brief moment,
I am aware that I am lost.

A brilliant flag waves proudly at half mast,
well-lit on a backdrop of tepid night sky,
as light pours from library windows
like golden dominos, shoulder to shoulder.

I stand on a compass, whose needle faithfully points
toward someone else's North,
and then I realize that I know not
where I am, or where I am going.

And yet, when peers ask me
what life has in store,
I cast a knowing grin,
and crease my shoulders.

title: Inkwell
content:
As I striketh my desk
As the ink streaks along
My hands are both broken
As they've been for long

My left hand is shattered
My right hand is frail
My mind is a tempest
My head like a veil

My thumbs weave like arrows
Their nocks all astrung
For all they can render
Are words often sung

Still young and still learning
Still looking for reason
We idly watch
As the sun changes season

And try as I might
To write senseful scrawl
The words on my paper
Mean nothing at all

title: Kronos
content:
There is not much I can say,
no narrative I can conjure,
that the Greeks did not better tell
when they spoke of the Titan King Kronos,

who ruled over time
with hands sharp and true,
and commanded the skies
to tick black and blue.

His children, the gods,
were emblems for man
and his wretched disposition
for hubris and arrogance,

unaware of the spirits
of nature that guide him
through a hostile universe
of uncertain pose.

And Kronos, fearing his demise
at the hands of his young children,
elected to consume them
with jaws mighty and ruthless,

unaware that as he selfishly cannibalized
the heirs to his kingdom,
so too did he inspire
the vengeful hand of Zeus,

who dethroned his father
and wore his pronged crown,
as time's iron fist
subsided to thunder.

And thus, the Greek gods
are no realer than mithril,
for they conquered time
and cast it to Tartarus,

the dungeon abyss,
a prison for Titans,
deep, deep, deep down below,
beneath Hades and Styx.

And we, as mere mortals,
are losing the war
against ages and eons
and time's bitter hand,

a war of attrition
where soldiers march forth
as they spoon-dig beneath them
their moon-shadowed graves.

If only we had
the strength and the valor
to stare down the tyrant
and challenge his throne,

we could write a million novels,
earn a thousand grand trophies,
break bread and eat olives
with our neighbors forever;

we could plant our soft footprints
on every mere inch
of this well-worn-out planet
and then do it again;

we could taste a true freedom
that sinks with the breeze
as it wafts its wispy way
from Olympus to Cairo.

Instead, we abate
in our fight against Kronos
as he chews and digests
our blood, flesh, and humor,

because we are not gods,
nor will we be ever,
and always will we succumb
to the halting hand of time.

title: Money Is That Warm Thing That Buys Cold Regret
content:
*I wrote this poem from the back of an Uber out of Worcester, Massachusetts, enroute to the Boston Logan Airport.

Money is that warm thing that buys cold regret
It steams and it smolders with blood, must, and sweat

It hisses and stings as onlookers pass by
While plaster monuments beckon, "Care for a try?"

Bonanzas galore, the offerings unbounded
Praying like vultures on fake fears, unfounded

But do not feel tempted to cast your due blame,
For the rich man knows better the rules of his game

"An eye for an eye", says the rich man, with glee
As atop a near-mountain of pupils sits he

"An eye for an arm", the meek peasant bids back,
For his brawny, hard labor dresses his broken back

"Hear, hear!" cheers the rich man, as he knows quite well
The peasant is burdened with debt and dispel

It would only be months--nay, weeks--'til he'd see
His small share of profit, washed away to the sea

And there he'd be left with quite less than before
Dejected, neglected, the poorest of poor

So listen, dear stranger, and don't you forget:
Money is that warm thing that buys cold regret.

title: Pentecostal Nightmares
content:
* This poem is about the impending crisis presented by artificial intelligence (the Zephyr), spearheaded by techno-industrialists who promise economic upheaval without a coherent plan for the alternative.

A zealous green Zephyr caresses the Earth,
leaving mildewy musk in its woeful wake.

Meek men hunch on shoulders of disparate giants,
their brawny hands bruised from heavyweight brawls,
while surface-born darlings sit dangling from doorways
and trod head-slung in their tenement halls,
their feet cold and bare amidst winter's defiance.

The wind carries promise to beckon an era
of lazy-cat sun-naps and pompous peruse,
yet offers no solace for withering purpose
but the tender indulgence of whiskey-brown booze.

And when the Pentecostal nightmares have abated,
what will remain but the smithering delinquency of yesteryear?

title: Quality and Medium
content:
I've hitherto wondered,
How much I've refined.
Today I am polished,
like shoes freshly shined.

But yesterday notice,
acknowledge the noise,
to uncouth behavior, say,
"Boys will be boys."

At birth I look upward
I take in what's shown
But in times of conflict
I feel all alone

I measure my angle
I calibrate gently
But scope is confined
By life's silver century

Ignore every semblance
of sanity's reason,
for hours mature
as forests change season.

What early brought verdant
at present is browning,
tomorrow dismembered,
the trees all a-frowning.

Throughout my short years
I have wandered and wondered
I've heeded the weather
As shrouded skies thundered.

But when grave is near
After years of rehearsin'
I cannot ignore
That I'm still the same person.

title: Sandstone
content:
The sandstone preserves,
The memorable tales,
Of storms and of fires,
Of foresty trails.

The bluffs by the ocean,
They tower so high,
But onlookers caution,
As they wander by.

The stones serve a purpose,
To tell stories told,
To write down our history,
The new and the old.

They tell of the future,
Of tumbling boulders,
Of life's unsung burdens,
Too heavy to shoulder.

Together lie past, present, future, alike,
From slate-battered tombstones, to baby's first bike,
Contained in the sandstone that watches the world,
An eon of sorrow, unraveled, unfurled.

My past is for certain,
My wrongs and my rights,
But future's dark curtain,
Shrouds uncertain nights.

I know where I've been,
Time's river is flowing,
So beseech I the sandstone,
"Where am I going?"

title: Six Feet of Earth
content:
An endless ocean stretches wearily to a static horizon.
A towering bluff overlooks the war-torn seastrand.
Miles of barbed wire guard the shores from a long forgotten enemy.
Under six inches of earth, industrial fingerprints await an unlucky wanderer.

The call of uncivilized progress echoes on the hills.
Children scramble to return to their fathers and mothers.
Families trade a life on the surface for one in the underworld.
Under six feet of earth, the people funnel into the shelters they never hoped to occupy.

As the sun collides with the sea, another rises in its place.
A mighty tree quickly sprouts, fifteen miles off the Pacific shoreline.
The twilight sky is shrouded by a hazy silver ploom.
Under six feet of earth, all is silent.

title: Spent
content:
Ideas come often, they make hours whole
They give us a reason to focus control
They direct, they inspire, they break through the tolls,
They make us more human; together; whole.

But they evade my every attempt to grasp them
Except in the shallow waters before each night's rest
Surfacing only when they are certain to be forgotten.
They are who I am, and exist, they do not.

title: Sure-Fire
content:
Before me stretches not a path, but a continuous, many-angled wedge
My trajectory will be uncertain for many years hence
Alas I work and I tire, I indulge and perspire
With little to show recompense

Which way should I choose? Is it too late to turn?
Have the bridges of choice been set to a burn?
Will illusion and color help me to learn?
Or will I sit back 'til it is my turn?

The reaper waits not for those who relax,
For those who preach faith and those who preach facts,
The ones who're devoted to truth-to-the-max,
Who are stopped and forgotten on untrodden tracks

We lack what we're not and we have what we are
Though tidings of progress are less near than far
For we know all things good must come to an end
But of the converse we all do pretend

One needs a friend, with whom one can share
The ins and the outs, the why and the where
But the universe ignores our wherefore and tear,
For when we are watching, there's nobody there.

What is a rough path without the headlights ahead and the occasional glances behind?

title: The Boxer
content:
*I wrote this poem while listening to "The Boxer" by Carbon Leaf and then the very different homonymous
song recorded by Mumford & Sons, a cover of the original by Simon & Garfunkel.

Two by two, the soaring aspens
Fell to the dirt, sore and spent
Many were near, yet no sound was made
A call unheeded by deaf ears

In the foliage, spiders danced
And spun their webs with thorough care
But their silky homes were torn and stripped
By beings supreme and indifferent

Ragged in their homeworld
Now barren and forsaken
The critters of the forest
Seek refuge in what was taken

Never again to see beyond
The snow-kissed mountaintops
Their peaks breaking softly
Against a sky of fluffy white

A crisis in disguise

title: The Charm of the Highway Strip
content:
*This poem is named after the eponymous album by the Magnetic Fields.

What is it
About that saffron-speckled line
That calls to me,
Beckoning?

I turn the brass key
And toggle my headlights
As I make my hurried way
To God-only-knows-where

Silver headlights flash and flicker
Between soft plastic pylons
That maintain the illusion
Of separation

Ruby brakelights abruptly spark
Like jagged crimson daggers
A warning to heed something
Lying ahead

And then there is stillness
Like chromium steel
And the city's heart stops beating
For a brief moment

For a while, I am alone
Between the coarse gravel shoulder
And that golden highway strip
That keeps me from veering

Into pitch-black horizons
Not sure where it's taking me
But knowing
That soon,
I will be there.

title: The Lake of the Universe
content:
A thousand silver orbs trickle down a frozen plane
Dark clouds overhead offer their tentative foresight
The sky offers a halfhearted apology to the drowning flowers of the valley
The flowers respond with hesitant gratitude, disguising their frowns with uneasy smiles

An overflowing lake reflects a figure dark and small
The figure gazes into the visions of the future offered by the smooth and icy surface
The ice cracks, splitting the silhouette on a jagged axis
The rain continues to fall without the help of the apprehensive figure

A mighty celestial column betrays the gloom
The valley is illuminated by the graceful warriors of the sky
The flowers rejoice in the generous sunlight that they are now afforded
The pulse of the Earth is restored as the valley awaits the first Autumn breeze

The ice in the lake melts at the sight of the day star's gift
The day-blind moon rises in approval of its mother's gesture
An eternal summer lies ahead for the two halves of the figure,
united at last in the Lake of the Universe.

title: The Place Where There Is No Darkness
content:
A brilliant satin shield protects the silent whispers of the night
An urban gradient taints the moon's enveloping cloak
The sky is blind to the stars, who hide behind an unwavering mask
Their light waiting behind a contrast-breaking curtain

The stars only dare to shine when there is nothing left to be seen
In a desert, on a plain, only miles and miles from the world
On no crowded street can one catch a glimpse of the heavens, shimmering overhead
For the city is as oblivious to the stars as a cartoon is to the viewer

title: The Remarkable Tendency of Things to Get Wet
content:
*I wrote this poem while sitting outside the Bell Pavilion in the misty morning rain, sipping coffee and counting the raindrops as they gathered in puddles. At the time, there was an ongoing Teamsters labor union strike at San Diego State University, and one could hear their chants as they marched across campus throughout the week.

As the clouds pour down their pitter-patter tears,
To a grateful and mournful Earth,
Early spring flowers spring up from their soggy roots,
To remind you what they're worth.

Cheers arise from a chorus of poppies,
Burning like foxfire on a misty green knoll,
While the highway hums with frustration and ire,
As damp, seasoned pavement collects its grim toll.

Bare tables are sticky with fresh-fallen dew,
And puddles collect at the sole of my shoe,
How deep--no, how shallow--these waters must be,
Like a brackish lagoon where a river meets sea

The palm trees stand furrowed, disposed for a rest,
While underpaid workers do strike and protest,
Their banners and flags strewn high in the air,
For an audience that won't even pretend to care.

All this makes me wonder, before I forget,
Is this really all? Do we get what we get?
So here do I sit, and here I reflect,
On the remarkable tendency of things to get wet.

title: Thoughts from the Aeroplane Bound for Dixie
content:
*This work represents my first attempt at what one might call "semi-automatic writing", wherein I essentially wrote down the first thing that came to mind. I wrote this while cruising at 35,000 feet over the desert southwest on a flight from San Diego to New Orleans (stop in Dallas). In summary, this work reflects my thoughts and feelings as I gazed out the window and daydreamed about my unfamiliar destination. In particular, my attention was captured by the clouds, farms, and canyon-crawled desert landscape sprawled out below me.

Hazy hues of summer seeds sown early in the season
scatter hastily above fractal mountains
and beneath my tender feet.

Panels of plight deck sand-kissed plains
where rigid hands perform treacherous work,
afraid, afraid of the storm that might never come.

A blanched, billowing border bisects and maims the divided land,
with an arbitrary air of impatient whimsy at its heel.

My nose itches as it inches and knows not where it is bound,
but for a false human name which it had been given
out of spite for the valley it had tarnished.

Aching canyons open their wounds
to show me the veins and sinew
that keep its blood flowing,
confiding in me their superficial secrets.

I clock a map and read the time from a magnet,
a circuit, a demon of the digital age,
thumping with ceaseless information,
but revealing very little.

The Earth opens her quarry-mouth wide
and offers her rich stone,
a haphazard gift
from a mother to her dying children.

A fall, to be sure, awaits me in Dallas,
but again I will sink to the lowly green skies,
their cottonwood hue trading moonlight for stardom.

And fall again I will,
to the wicked blue bayous of New Orleans,
where little awaits me but stories untold.

And as I stare at the silver strands
of cottonball whiskey,
between deisel-born tip-taps
and screen-age pitter patter,
I am reminded at once
of a moment that never came.

One at which life seemed like a marble on a hill,
vastly outworn by the twisted ides of tall grass
and gravity beneath its silky swirls
and solitary see-through sheen.

Like a die spinning wildly in the air,
I sat there, momentarily at rest,
and yet moving decisively against my will.

Life, it seems, is not fooled by "could have" or "would have",
these dirty lies we tell ourselves to escape the droll;
Instead, the dictates of fate conspire
to constantly deprive us of the illusion we call being.

Like an anxious electron,
you may know where you are,
or you may know where you are going,
but never both.

Cast down your bucket and see for yourself,
lest your reddish eyes be blinded
by the troubling tail lights of tomorrow.

title: Turtle
content:
A fat turtle loiters on a sun-baked stone
His shell a bleak brown-gray
Dry for a while, between lazy laps and nameless naps
His daily sojourn in a careless paradise.

As my eyes drift, a wild fence lizard catches my gaze,
Her soft-scaled torso gently oscillating with her breath,
As she scrambles and peers from a piecemeal ledge,
Keeping always on her feet, unable to sit still.

The leaves, as they sail to the Earth, leave ripples when they land in the pond,
Disturbing the elder koi as she patrols the shallow depths.
She sustains herself on water-bed greens and scraps from her royal subjects,
As she evades the snapping jaws and airborne claws that embattle her home.

And then I know,
As the turtle burrows into his hard rock home,
Beneath the half-broken shade of a winter-thinned tree,
And as the lizard traipses along the warm cobblestone wall,
Where only keenly-honed eyes can see,
And as the koi swims in hostile circuits,
Yearning for the blue-steel sea,
That I am one with the sedentary turtle, with the swift-legged lizard, and with the battle-scarred koi,
And that when I observe them,
With eyes glassy and naive,
I am looking at me.
